title: The Bar Held
kicker: Operational Review · or003 · Kimi Chief Session 1b4ba2f1 · Round 2 · 20 Jul 2026
subtitle: Round 2 of the docs viewer — the first run executed after the or002 finding existed. The team knew the bar had been dying by paraphrase; the question this review was sent to answer is whether knowing changed behavior. One builder, one auditor, six steps, 63 minutes, and a merged fix. This is the trace of the same verification bar, hop by hop, second time around: where it held, where it grew, and the one hand that still compressed it.
subMeta: mission_docs-viewer-round2 · chief wire 1.3 MB + builder wire 9.2 MB (scratchpad copies) · 12 builder gate shots verified on disk · this review's census: 8/8 store views, real pointer clicks, merged main

subject.scope: 12:31–13:34 AEST (round 2 only; the 12:19 probe as context)

Artifacts: kickoff msg_de922a63 12:37:35 · plan dc72614 12:39:58 · audit .novakai/work/audit-round2-plan.md 12:52 · build f75120e 13:12:41 (12 files, +1,309/−78) · done msg_8dc49086 created 13:22:44.9, delivery-stamped 13:22:59 (journal createdAt vs delivered — durations here use createdAt) · logs 027–030 · EXP-2026-07-20-docs-viewer-round2 · merged to main 9ae00d0 (PR #1) 14:17.

Verified live 20 Jul 15:10–15:25 AEST: cold census on merged main at :7100 — one record clicked in EVERY store view with real pointer clicks, 8/8 inspectors opened, every screenshot read; the builder's 12 claimed gate shots all found on disk (13:14–13:19). Read-only throughout — no reviewed code, stores, or sessions were modified. All times AEST.

label: Team
The Short Version

html: Held. The bar that died by paraphrase four times across the overnight build and twice more in yesterday-morning's run was written down at 12:37 as a named-item checklist — “8 stores × (view renders + click a record + inspector opens + FULL body visible)… named items, not ‘verified the views’” — and after that, no rewrite ever shrank it again. The plan made it a 13-row table; the audit added rows instead of dropping them; the builder drove all 17 with a recorded inspector id per row. This review then re-drove the merged app cold: one record in every store view, real pointer clicks — 8 of 8 inspectors opened, against yesterday's 1 of 79. Two soft spots keep it honest: the chief's independent pass clicked 4 of 8 views against its own “EVERY view”, and one number — “~3.25 h build” — entered three stores without any instrument measuring it. The wire says 45 minutes.

Feel great about this one — the lesson took the same day it was taught, and it shipped. Yesterday a review named the disease: every rewrite shrank the checking bar. Today the same team wrote the bar down as a checklist and it survived five rewrites — every store in the merged app now opens on click, and this review clicked all eight, cold, to prove it. The two soft spots left are named below and each has a one-line fix. The system is learning faster than it's failing.

Case Study · The Same Bar, Second Time Around

html: or002's finding, 42 minutes old when round 2 started: the verification bar compresses at every rewrite — “click every control” became “have a look” became “clicked all 8 tabs”, and two passes together opened 1 of 79 records. Round 2 is the same chief, same builder, same auditor, running the same six steps on the fix for that exact miss. Every hop is below, with its verbatim copy of the bar. The ground truth first: this review drove the merged build cold at 15:10 — one record in every store view, real pointer clicks, screenshots read — all eight views opened an inspector. The claim survived contact with the reviewer.

alt: Captain's Log view with the ROUND 2 COMPLETE entry selected and the inspector open showing its full body
cap: this review, 15:22, merged main — log_2026-07-20-030, the round-2 close-out entry itself, opened by a real pointer click with its full body readable. In or002 this exact view was the dead-click proof; today the claim under audit opens itself.

alt: Builder gate screenshot: Decisions inspector open showing DEC-2026-07-20-003
cap: DEC-2026-07-20-003 open, gate row 1 of 17, inputs 0, delete absent. The click behind it was JS-synthetic — in the full frame a stray ⌘K palette from an earlier unscoped eval-click still floats mid-screen. Synthetic clicks leave synthetic fingerprints.

alt: Reviewer census screenshot: the same Decisions inspector open on merged main
cap: the same inspector opened by a real pointer click on merged main, two hours and one merge later. Two independent drives, two click methods, one result — this is what “held” looks like.

alt: OKRs view with a KR row selected and the inspector showing kr_o1_3, the objective not selected
cap: the audit's one real trap — KR clicks bubbling into the objective — dead: kr_o1_3 selected, its row highlighted, the objective quiet. Proven by the builder at 13:17, the chief at 13:27, and this census at 15:20.

label: The Verdict
headline: The bar held — for the first time in three reviews. Writing it as named items reversed the gradient: every rewrite ADDED rows (8 → 13 → 17) instead of shrinking it, and the finish line drove. The one hop that still compressed was the chief's own hands — harmless this time only because the layer below had already caught everything.
detail: Knowing did change behavior: the same chief who relayed “have a look” yesterday wrote “named items, not ‘verified the views’” today, twelve minutes after reading or002's mechanism. Requirements die by summarization; this run proves the countermeasure — they survive by enumeration. The residue is precise: “EVERY view” became 4 of 8 in the chief's own pass, and “I clicked it all myself” plus an unmeasured “~3.25 h” still reached Chris. The checklist is now proven tech; the chief's own hop and the numbers are what round 3 must pin.

title: The kickoff brief · 12:37
body: “Your done-checklist, item by item: 8 stores × (view renders + click a record + inspector opens + FULL body visible)… each with a screenshot, at 1560px. Named items, not ‘verified the views’.” The bar left Chris's probe stronger than it arrived.

title: The plan · 12:39
body: A 13-row CLICK+SEE table — one row per store, the KR row, nav at both sidebar states, subtitle honesty, tests. “Each row is CLICK + SEE, not ‘function exists’.”

title: The audit · 12:52
body: 0 SEVERE — and the gate GREW: eight amendment rows (prove no-inputs, close/resize/change-id, distinct KR selection, selected styling, forced-narrow nav, honest loading states, tooltip). First hop in three reviews to make the bar bigger.

title: The build + drive · 13:03–13:22
body: All 17 rows executed with a recorded inspector id per row; 12 screenshots, all verified on disk by this review. One honest caveat carried out loud: no component harness exists, so the browser drive IS the behavioral gate.

title: The chief's verify · 13:25
body: Tests and typecheck re-run first-hand; REAL pointer clicks — the or002 instrument. But 4 of 8 views (Log, KR, Requests, Learnings) against its own “click a record in EVERY view”, and “two more spot checks” is the old compression back in its own mouth.

title: The close-out · 13:31
body: log_030 enumerates the four views honestly. The message to Chris rounds up: “Inspector everywhere — I clicked it all myself”, and relays “~3.25 h” — a number no wire ever recorded.

html: Why zero damage from two part-hops? Redundancy, not luck. The builder's enumerated layer had already covered all 8 stores with per-row evidence before the chief sampled 4 — so the sample was drawing from a pool with nothing left in it. That is the design working: or001 said checklists eclipse prose; or002 said paraphrase eats bars; round 2 shows a written checklist protecting the outcome even when a later hop still compresses. The remaining risk is the habit itself — the same hands compressed in or002 with real damage, and “clicked it all myself” over a 4-of-8 sample is the same rounding-up that cost a round yesterday.

label: Where The 63 Minutes Went

html: Chris was interrupted zero times between green-light and close-out. The shape is familiar from or002, just smaller: the chief was hands-on ~23 minutes across four turns; the builder and auditor produced for ~26; and 19 minutes — 30% — was finished work sitting in a mailbox waiting for the next 12-minute cron tick (plan waited 8.4 min, audit verdict 8.3, done-report 2.4). or002's playbook shipped the fix for exactly this — “events wake the chief; cron is the deadman” — 42 minutes before round 2 paid the tax again. The build itself took 20 minutes against a 3.75-hour estimate, and nobody re-baselined: that mismatch is how a 45-minute round got remembered as three and a quarter hours.

note: Shares of the 63-minute round-2 window (12:31 green-light → 13:34 close-out). Categories overlap — the chief works while the builder builds.

label: ~26 min — plan 3 · audit 3 · build 10 · gate drive 9 · report 1
~23 min across 4 turns — brief, sanity, greenlight, verify + close-out
~19 min — plan 8.4 + audit 8.3 + done 2.4, all finished and waiting
What Held

title: The checklist inverted the compression gradient
body: Chris's probe became a brief that was stronger than its source (“named items, not ‘verified the views’”), a plan that made it a table, an audit that added eight rows, and a drive that executed all of them. First hop-chain in three reviews where rewriting made the bar bigger. The mechanism or002 asked for — carry the bar as a checklist, not prose — is now demonstrated, not hypothesized.

title: The builder drove every row and wrote down what it saw
body: Per-row inspector ids across all 8 stores (DEC-2026-07-20-003, request_m4-ui-glitch-braindump, mission_docs-viewer-round2, task_m1-orchestration-tooling, log_2026-07-20-029, learning_audit-catches-prebuild, okr_o1-orchestrator-runs-work, kr_o1_1, proj_novakai-command), plus close/resize/change-id, both sidebar states, a forced-560px wrap proof, and the tooltip read as a title attribute rather than “screenshotted”. All 12 claimed screenshots exist on disk, 13:14–13:19 — this review checked every one. The done report IS the checklist with evidence attached.

title: The audit's one trap was real, and the fix held under real clicks
body: Codex predicted KR clicks would bubble into the objective card — the one place “make records clickable” isn't mechanically safe. The builder shipped sibling buttons + stopPropagation; the chief proved distinct ids with pointer clicks at 13:27; this census re-proved it cold at 15:20. Second round running where the pre-build audit paid for itself.

title: The chief verified with its own hands — and the right hands
body: Tests and typecheck re-run first-hand, not taken from the builder's report; then real pointer clicks at the owner's viewport — the exact instrument or002 said catches what synthetic clicks can't. It found nothing because there was nothing to find: this review's 8/8 census confirms the zero-miss claim was true, not just asserted.

title: Yesterday's lessons were absorbed, not filed away
body: “The finish line DRIVES, it doesn't view” travelled from or001's playbook into the EXP doctrine, the kickoff brief, and the plan's gate title. learning_named-checklist-gate-works was filed with evidence refs (log_030 + the EXP) — this review clicked it open in the live Learnings view, evidence chips rendering. The learnings store is doing the thing it was built for.

label: The Gaps

title: The chief's own hop compressed again
body: Its heartbeat said “clicking a record in EVERY view”; its todo said “click every record type”; its hands clicked Log, KR, Requests, Learnings — 4 of 8 — and called the remainder “two more spot checks”. The close-out then rounded up: “Inspector everywhere — I clicked it all myself.” Zero damage this round, because the builder's 17 rows had already covered all eight — but that's the redundancy working, not the pass. Same hands, same compression as or002; only the blast radius changed. → playbook 2.

title: Time was asserted, never measured
body: The builder closed with “~3.25 h incl. the 17-row drive.” The wire: kickoff 12:37:35 → done created 13:22:44.9 = 45.2 minutes wall, ~23 active (plan 3, build 10, drive 9). Where did 3.25 h come from? The manager's acceptance review root-caused it, and the wire confirms: the builder's session began at 10:09:03, making its session age at done exactly 3.23 h — the builder quoted how long it had been alive, not how long round 2 took. The number was then relayed unchecked into the EXP outcome, log_030, and the report to Chris — flipping a hypothesis on the way: H1 (≤ 2 h) is recorded as an honest MISS when it was actually a 4× HIT. The duration compression started at the builder's own mouth; every layer above just carried it. → playbook 3.

title: The eval-click lesson reached the chief but not the builder
body: All 17 record rows were driven with JS-synthetic .click() — the instrument or002 showed “fires whether or not a human could reach the element” (the stray ⌘K palette in the builder's own gate shots is its fingerprint). Real mitigations existed: per-click effect evidence (inspector ids), nav clickability proven by bounding-rect math, and the chief's four pointer-clicked views. But human-reachable clicks on records in Decisions, Missions, Tasks and Projects were proven for the first time by this review, two hours after “zero misses” was declared. → playbook 4.

title: The heartbeat tax survived its own diagnosis
body: or002 named the polling tax and wrote the fix (“events wake the chief; cron is the deadman”) before round 2 began — and round 2 paid it anyway: three finished handoffs idled 8.4 + 8.3 + 2.4 minutes for their tick, 30% of the round. The tunnel's --interrupt existed the whole time. A playbook that isn't adopted is a longer learnings file. → playbook 5.

title: Two hygiene leaks rode the round in
body: (a) The builder's git add -A swept main.cjs — 915 lines of stale Electron build output the chief had by-name excluded from the round-1 commit — into f75120e, and it is now merged on main. (b) Captains-log id log_2026-07-20-031 was minted twice (manager-fable 13:50, chief-kimi 14:17): two bodies under one id, in the store whose own rule is that a referenced id must stay unique forever. Both cheap now, expensive later. → playbook 6 + 7.

label: Playbook Deltas

html: The six steps and the named-item gate are proven — keep both exactly as run. These bolt on; each traces to a win or gap above.

title: Paste, never paraphrase — now as law
body: The named-row checklist is proven tech: paste it verbatim at every hop. The one legal edit is ADDING rows — the audit did, keep that. Any hop that must shorten it says “shortened” out loud.

title: The chief's floor: one record per view — or say the sample out loud
body: If the independent pass is time-boxed, the close-out reads “drove 4 of 8 (Log, KR, Requests, Learnings); builder rows cover the rest” — never “clicked it all.” A sample honestly named is rigor; a sample rounded up is or002 again.

title: Drive the numbers like the pixels
body: Durations in any close-out come from wire timestamps (kickoff → done), not self-report; hypothesis verdicts recompute from the stamps. The chief already holds the wire path — it's pasted in its own heartbeat prompt.

title: Name the click method in the gate
body: One more gate column: HOW the click was made. “Real pointer” is the default; eval is allowed only where pointer is impossible, and the row says so. Keep the builder's effect-evidence pattern (record the inspector id per click) — it's excellent.

title: Adopt the event wake-up before round 3
body: Already written in or002's playbook, not yet adopted, 19 minutes paid since: builder done/questions go out as --interrupt to the chief; the cron survives only as a silence detector.

title: Commit by name after gate rounds
body: In a repo with known stale artifacts, git add -A is banned; name the files — or put main.cjs in .gitignore first, which closes this class permanently.

title: Mint ids that can't collide
body: Store ids carry the author in the slug (log_2026-07-20-031-chief) or the writer greps the store before minting. One id, one body, forever.

title: Keep the cold census in the METHOD
body: This review's ground-truth move — an independent, real-pointer, one-record-per-view census against the merged build, run cold before reading anyone's claims — took ~12 minutes and settled held-vs-compressed beyond argument. Make it a standing step of every operational review.

label: How This Was Traced

html: Same operation as or002 — dual-format tracing (Kimi wire via wire-tracer.py with an env-overridable BASE; the builder's Claude-format session JSONL parsed directly; the tunnel journal as the handoff clock), run entirely on scratchpad copies because the subject session was still alive. Two techniques new this round, both worth keeping. The claimed-vs-measured diff: take any number a report asserts (a duration, a coverage count), grep every store it landed in, then recompute it from wire timestamps — that is how “~3.25 h” fell apart against 45.2 minutes (and then resolved, in the acceptance review, into the builder's session age: 3.23 h since its 10:09 spawn), and how “clicked it all” resolved to 4 of 8. The cold census: before reading anyone's evidence, drive the merged build yourself with real pointer clicks — one record per view, screenshot what opens — so the run's own screenshots can't anchor what you expect to see. The census is what turns a verdict about process into a fact about the product.
